chunk_summary: Guidelines for vegetarian complementary feeding emphasize meal frequency, texture progression, and a minimum viable menu to support growth and development in infants aged 6–23 months, with a focus on energy-dense, iron-rich foods and responsive feeding practices.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of starting complementary feeding at 6 months with thick, energy-dense semi-solids.
Learn how to progress meal frequency and texture to support chewing skills and energy intake.
Identify a practical minimum viable menu for vegetarian feeding in Indian regional contexts.
Recognize the impact of eliminating empty-calorie foods and promoting responsive feeding.
key_facts: Complementary feeding should begin at 6 months with 1–2 thick meals/day, progressing to 3 meals/day by 9–11 months.
A minimum viable menu includes a cereal–pulse base (e.g., khichdi, daliya with moong, idli/dosa with sambar), a fat source, and a fruit/vegetable.
Texture progression is critical: thick mashed foods to soft lumps and finger foods as the child grows.
National surveys show very low minimum acceptable diet coverage in 6–23 month-olds.
Eliminating empty-calorie foods (e.g., biscuits, sweetened drinks) prevents poor dietary preferences later.
Document meal frequency and observable properties (e.g., portion size, thickness) to monitor progress.
reference_quote: Vegetarian complementary feeding is safest when aligned to standard milestones rather than delayed 'until the child is older.'
document_key_topics: Minimum viable menu for Indian regions
Impact of empty-calorie foods on diet quality
